context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Spider Diagram

Recommended due date: August 1 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content. For Certificate learners, the instructors will select a sample of submissions to summarize with feedback in an uploaded video. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners as a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will complete a Spider Diagram to map out climate action in your case study city as it relates to the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation.

Assignment Options
The Spider Diagram activity can be completed in two different ways, which will be described in detail on the following pages:

Option 1: Use the Spider Diagram table (see example on next pages) to write out the information you find about your city (preferred).
• To complete this option, you will copy and paste a table template into your submission

Option 2: Use the Spider Diagram diagram (see example on next pages) to draw out the information you find about your city.
• To complete this option, you will download, print and complete the map by hand, then scan it in to add it to your submission

After you complete the submission in one of the two ways above, you will also be required to participate in the discussion forum with your fellow learners. The prompt will be found on the pages that follow.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: idk i'm kinda lost on the spider diagram tbh. can u just explain the whole assignment from the start?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—let’s simplify it.

Your job is to map your case study city’s climate action onto the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation using either a table or a hand-drawn spider diagram.

Think of it in 4 parts:
1. Pick your case study city.
2. Find climate actions, plans, or policies in that city.
3. Sort what you find into the 5 pathways.
4. Submit the diagram/table, then do the discussion forum.

The key skill is not “drawing”—it’s organizing evidence about the city.

Start here: what city are you using, and have you already found any climate plan, sustainability plan, or resilience strategy for it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will briefly re-orient the student to the assignment, reduce overwhelm by chunking it into a few concrete steps, and then use guided questions to make the student identify their own city and next action. I will not complete the task for them or provide content for the diagram; instead I will structure the problem and ask for one decision that moves directly toward completion.

exchanges.student: uh maybe that's why i'm stuck lol, i don't even know what counts as a "pathway" rn. can u explain those first from scratch?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes. A “pathway” is basically a big category of urban change. The spider diagram asks: in what main ways is this city trying to transform itself for climate action?

So you are not listing random facts—you are sorting city actions into 5 buckets from the course.

Important: for this assignment, the exact names of the 5 pathways should come from your course materials. Do you have the page, lecture, or handout that lists them? If yes, paste the 5 names here and we’ll figure out what each one means.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will clarify the core concept the student is stuck on, but keep it concise and avoid doing the categorization for them. To preserve guided discovery, I’ll define the purpose of the pathways at a high level and then ask the student to recall or locate the course framing so they begin anchoring their own understanding to the assignment materials.

exchanges.student: i prob saw them before but rn i'm blanking fr. can u just give me a simple version of all 5 so i can tell what goes where?
exchanges.tutor:
I can help you interpret them, but I’m not going to replace the course’s official 5 with my own version—that would set you up badly.

What you need right now is the exact list from the course page. Once you paste the 5 names, we can do this fast: for each one, I’ll ask you what kind of city action seems to belong there.

Can you check the assignment page or lecture notes and send the 5 pathway titles exactly as written?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should avoid inventing or asserting specific pathway names if the assignment depends on course-defined categories the student must use. To stay accurate and within role, I’ll ask the student to retrieve the official list, while still giving a minimal conceptual frame for how to think about categories. This keeps the guidance useful but does not complete the task or risk misinformation.

exchanges.student: ugh ok but i'm still confused abt the overall thing tbh. like is this basically just summarizing the city's climate plan or is it deeper than that?
exchanges.tutor:
It’s more than a summary. You’re selecting evidence from the city and organizing it by the 5 pathways, so it’s a structured map of climate action, not a full narrative recap.

So ask yourself: what is this city doing, and which pathway does each action fit under?

What city are you working with? That’s the next thing we need fixed.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will reduce confusion by clarifying the assignment’s purpose at a high level without supplying content. I’ll contrast “summary” with “organized analysis” and then ask one focused question that moves the student toward action on their specific city.

exchanges.student: i haven't fully picked one yet lol, that's part of the problem. can u maybe tell me what makes a city easier for this assignment?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes. An easier city usually has:
1. a public climate action/resilience/sustainability plan,
2. clear city-government websites,
3. enough examples to fit several pathways,
4. information in a language you can read easily.

So don’t pick the “coolest” city—pick the most documentable one.

Give me 2–3 cities you’re considering, and we’ll test which is easiest.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is stuck at city selection, so I should guide them toward criteria for a workable case rather than naming or choosing a city for them. I will keep it bite-sized, practical, and ask them to generate options so they remain responsible for the decision.

exchanges.student: maybe nyc, copenhagen, or singapore? ngl i can't tell which one would be least messy.
exchanges.tutor:
Good options. Don’t overcomplicate this.

Compare them on just 3 things:
1. Can you quickly find an official city climate plan?
2. Does the city website clearly describe actions?
3. Do you already know enough context to avoid getting lost?

Which of those 3 feels easiest for you right now? Pick one based on ease, not prestige.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should not choose for the student outright, but I can direct them to evaluate the options using the criteria already established. I’ll ask comparative questions that move directly toward a decision and avoid unnecessary detail.

exchanges.student: probably nyc i guess, but i'm still kinda lost on what i'm actually supposed to put in each section tbh. can u walk me through like the whole flow once?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes. For NYC, the flow is:

1. Find 1–2 official sources about city climate action.
2. Skim for concrete actions, policies, plans, or targets.
3. Make a rough list of those actions.
4. Sort each action into one of the 5 course pathways.
5. Put short notes in the table/diagram for each pathway.
6. Notice gaps too—if one pathway has less evidence, that itself tells you something.
7. Then do the discussion post.

So the unit you’re putting in each section is not a paragraph—it’s brief evidence about what the city is doing.

What is one official NYC source you’ve found, or need help locating?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student now has a tentative city and needs procedural clarity. I will provide a concise step-by-step workflow for completing the spider diagram without filling in the content. I’ll keep it actionable and end with a question that prompts the student to start gathering evidence.

exchanges.student: i haven't found one yet tbh. can u just tell me what kind of stuff from nyc would even count as "brief evidence" so i know what i'm looking for?
exchanges.tutor:
“Brief evidence” means a specific, documentable city action—not vague praise like “NYC cares about climate.”

What counts:
- a named plan or policy
- a target or deadline
- a program the city runs
- a regulation or building rule
- an infrastructure project
- an agency/office responsible

So look for phrases like: adopted, launched, requires, invests in, targets, commits to.

What official NYC webpage or PDF can you open first: climate plan, sustainability plan, or resilience plan?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will give the student a compact definition of what counts as evidence, with generic examples of evidence types rather than NYC-specific content, so I avoid doing the assignment for them. I will keep it bite-sized and end with a question that pushes them to search for an official source.

exchanges.student: idk, i'm still overwhelmed rn. can u just give me a super basic example of how one item would look in the table?
exchanges.tutor:
Fine—just the format, not the content.

One table item might look like:
- Pathway: [course pathway name]
- Evidence: [name of city policy/plan/program]
- Note: [1 short phrase on what it does]

Example shape:
- Pathway: [X]
- Evidence: “City plan/program name”
- Note: sets a target / changes a rule / funds a project

Now do the real step: find one official NYC source and pull one named action from it. What source will you check first?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is overwhelmed, so I should reduce cognitive load with a very minimal, non-city-specific example of table structure rather than a content answer. I must avoid completing the assignment or giving pathway-specific content, so I’ll show the form of an entry using placeholders and then ask the student to produce one from an official source.

exchanges.student: ok maybe i'll check the nyc climate plan first, but tbh can u also remind me what the assignment wants at the end bc i'm mixing up the table, diagram, and forum stuff.
exchanges.tutor:
Yes. The assignment has 2 deliverables:

1. Main submission:
- either the table version OR the hand-drawn spider diagram
- both do the same job: organize your city’s climate actions by the 5 pathways

2. Discussion forum:
- a separate participation piece after you complete the diagram/table

So: choose NYC, find evidence, sort it into the 5 pathways, submit in one format, then do the forum.

Don’t dwell on format yet. Which format feels easier for you: table or diagram?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will briefly untangle the deliverables and sequence them clearly, since the student is overwhelmed and needs orientation. I’ll keep it concise, focused on the assignment only, and end with one small next step to maintain guided discovery without doing the work for them.
